title: Cinnamon rolls
subtitle: American-style enriched rolls with brown sugar filling and cream cheese icing
excerpt: Nine large cinnamon rolls baked touching in a tin, each one a spiral of enriched dough wrapped around a brown sugar and cinnamon filling. Finished with a cream cheese icing applied while the rolls are still warm so it melts into the surface.
sourceNotes: Enriched, spiced rolled buns are documented across European baking traditions from the seventeenth century onward: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg, describes spiced enriched doughs rolled with butter and sugar. The American cinnamon roll, with a brown-sugar-and-cinnamon filling and a cream-based icing, is documented in American domestic cookbooks from the early twentieth century, many available via the Internet Archive, where the rolled-and-cut method and the use of cream cheese in the icing are both recorded.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before icing. Defrost at room temperature, then warm in a 160°C oven for 8 minutes before icing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After cutting and arranging in the tin, cover and refrigerate overnight. Bring to room temperature for 45 minutes before baking. Make the icing while the rolls bake.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The enriched dough colours faster than a lean loaf — reduce to 170°C if the tops are catching before 20 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: An icing made from cream cheese beaten with icing sugar and vanilla. Less sweet and more tangy than a standard buttercream, it cuts through the richness of an enriched dough. Applied while the rolls are still warm so it softens into the surface rather than setting into a hard shell.

content.text: A cinnamon roll is an enriched dough spread with butter, brown sugar, and cinnamon, rolled into a tight log, cut into rounds, and baked touching in a tin. The icing goes on warm from the oven so it softens rather than sets. The nine rolls baked together fill the tin and pull apart at the table.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and the {{caster-sugar}} for the dough in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} for the dough and whisk into the {{whole-milk}} for the dough. Pour into the flour and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 5 minutes, then add the {{unsalted-butter}} for the dough in four additions, working each piece in before adding the next. The dough will feel greasy at first, then smooth. Knead for a further 5 minutes until glossy and elastic. It will be quite soft.
Cover and leave in a warm place for 90 minutes until roughly doubled.

Butter the tin. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough out to a rectangle about 45 × 35 cm. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} for the filling with the {{light-brown-sugar}} and {{cinnamon-ground}} until smooth, then spread evenly over the dough, leaving a 2 cm border along one long edge. Roll the dough tightly from the opposite long edge into a log about 45 cm long. Pinch the border edge to seal. Cut the log into 9 equal rounds (each about 5 cm thick) and arrange cut-side up in the tin in a 3×3 grid.
Cover loosely and leave for 60 minutes until the rolls are puffed and touching. Preheat the oven to 180°C fan.

Bake for 25–28 minutes until the tops are deep amber and a probe reads 90°C in the centre roll. While the rolls bake, make the icing: beat the {{cream-cheese}} until smooth, then beat in the sifted {{icing-sugar}} and {{vanilla-extract}} until you have a thick, spreadable icing. This is the
. As soon as the rolls come out of the oven, spread it over the hot surface. Leave in the tin for 10 minutes before serving.
